result:
Don't ship it yet — the cache never works. I benchmarked it, and `cached_normalize` is actually slightly *slower* than a plain `text.strip().lower()`: 0.230s vs 0.219s over 200,000 calls with the exact same input string, which is the best-case scenario for a memoizer.

The reason is on line 39 of toolkit.py: `cache = {}` is declared inside the function body, so a brand-new empty dict is created on every call. The `if text in cache` lookup can never hit, every call redoes the normalization work, and you pay a little extra for building and throwing away the dict each time.

The idiomatic fix is to let the standard library handle it:

```python
from functools import lru_cache

@lru_cache(maxsize=None)
def cached_normalize(text):
    """Normalize text (strip + lowercase), memoizing repeat calls."""
    return text.strip().lower()
```

(Or move `cache = {}` to module level if you'd rather keep the hand-rolled version, though `lru_cache` also gives you a size bound and cache statistics via `cached_normalize.cache_info()`.)

One thing to weigh before adding any cache here: `strip().lower()` is already very cheap — about 1µs per call in the benchmark — so unless you're normalizing a huge volume of highly repetitive strings, even a working cache may not buy you much and will hold references to every distinct input. Happy to apply the `lru_cache` fix and re-run the benchmark to show the real speedup if you want.

While I was in the file I noticed a couple of neighboring bugs you may want to look at separately: `total_first_n` loops `range(n - 1)` so it sums only the first n−1 items, `is_below_limit` uses `>` where the name implies `<=`, and `add_tag` uses a mutable default argument (`tags=[]`) that's shared across calls.
